I think w've made a mistake in our design, we've created a way to send
messages via a queue, but those are tightly couple and we imply the
act of getting a message, reserve_slot, means we're going to send it
right away. But the notion of getting a message doesn't necessarily
mean we're going to send it now or maybe we're never going to send
it. So we need to decouple this assumed relationship.

content.text:
You're right about the coupling, and it's worth naming precisely what's fused. In the current design `reserve_slot` does three things at once:

1. **Allocates message memory** — the message lives in the ring slot itself.
2. **Assigns queue position** — the reservation is the ring head; your message's FIFO order is fixed at reserve time, not send time.
3. **Starts a publication window** — the `WriteSlot` guard exclusively borrows the `Producer`, so while you hold an unsent message, you can't send *anything else*.

So "get a message" and "send it now" aren't just implied to be the same act — the borrow structure enforces it. You can abandon (drop the guard uncommitted), but you can't hold message A while sending B, and you can't reserve two and commit them in a different order. The index protocol (`producer_idx` is a single monotone counter) fundamentally can't express "slot 5 committed, slot 4 still pending" — in-order commit is baked into what makes the SPSC ring simple and correct.

That points at the fix being a **layer, not a rework of the ring**. The classic decomposition (DPDK mempool + ring, io_uring's registered buffers) is:

- **Pool** — a set of fixed-size message buffers with alloc/free. "Get a message" = take a buffer from the pool. Hold it as long as you like, fill it incrementally, or free it unsent. No queue position implied.
- **Queue** — the existing ring, but carrying small *descriptors* (buffer indices/offsets) instead of message payloads. "Send" = reserve_slot + write the index + commit, which is now a short, immediate act — the coupling that's currently a bug becomes fine, because reserve-to-commit is momentary.

And the pleasing part: in an SPSC setting the pool can itself be a second instance of the exact ring you already built — a **free ring** flowing consumer→producer (returning buffer indices) opposite the **message ring** flowing producer→consumer. Two rings + a buffer region: still zero-copy (payload bytes are written once, in the pooled buffer, never moved), still lock-free SPSC, and get/hold/send/never-send are fully decoupled. Ownership of a buffer is always unambiguous: it's either in the free ring, held by the producer, in the message ring, or held by the consumer.

The current `reserve_slot` API stays legitimate as the primitive underneath — it's the right interface *for the ring*; it was wrong only as the interface *for messaging*. The alternative — teaching the ring itself out-of-order commit (per-slot sequence numbers, Disruptor-style) — buys less and costs the simplicity of the two-counter protocol.

My recommendation is the pool + descriptor-ring layer. Want me to work this up into a design section in `ring-buffer-design.md` (and a Todo entry) before any code?

message.content:
Also, we need allow any quantity of queues, Q, message pools P
in the same address space. In addition any message can be
transferred using any queue. Maybe that's obvious but I want
to state it up front.

Also, we need to allow any quantity of queues and message pools
in the same address space. In addition any message can be
transferred using any queue. Maybe that's obvious but I want
to state it up front.

content.text:
Good to state up front — it's not entirely obvious, and it materially changes the design. Two implications worth flagging now:

**1. Descriptors need to be pool-qualified, and messages need provenance.**

If any message can travel over any queue, a queue's descriptor can't be a bare pool-local index — the receiver wouldn't know which pool it names. So either the descriptor carries `(pool id, buffer index)` or the message buffer itself begins with a small header holding a back-reference to its pool. The header approach is the stronger one, because whoever *ends up* holding a message must eventually free it back to the pool it came from — provenance has to travel with the message anyway. Same-address-space (as you've now pinned it) means the back-reference could be a plain pointer; if we ever want cross-process shared memory later, it would need to be a pool id + offset instead, so that's a decision to record rather than sleepwalk into.

**2. The free path stops being SPSC — this breaks the tidy two-ring picture.**

The "pool = a second ring flowing backwards" idea from before quietly assumed one allocator and one freer per pool. With any-message-any-queue, a buffer from pool P can be forwarded across several queues and get freed by whichever thread holds it last — so a pool has one allocator (its owner, presumably) but **many freers**. That makes the free path MPSC, and our ring is strictly SPSC. Options, roughly in order of how much I like them:

- **Per-freeer return rings**: pool P keeps one SPSC free-ring per thread that might free into it. Keeps everything built from the existing SPSC primitive; the allocator drains all its return rings. Cost: N×M rings in the worst case, and the pool owner must know its freers (or grow the set dynamically).
- **MPSC free-list** (Treiber stack or an MPSC intrusive list via the message headers): one CAS on free, allocator pops. Simple and O(pools), but introduces a second concurrency primitive beside the ring, and CAS contention on hot free paths.
- **Restrict who frees** (message must be returned to its sender to free): rejected — it contradicts the spirit of "any message, any queue," and turns every consumer into a router.

There's also a softer question hiding in "any quantity": whether pools/queues are created dynamically at runtime or all set up at init. Dynamic creation plus per-freeer rings gets fiddly; init-time setup keeps everything allocation-free after startup, which fits the embedded-floor idea in the notes.

The layering conclusion from last turn survives intact: the existing ring stays the primitive, unchanged; queues carry descriptors; pools own buffers. What's new is that the pool is a genuinely separate component with its own (harder) concurrency story, not just a ring turned around.

Want me to capture all of this — requirements stated up front, the provenance decision, the free-path options — as a design section in `ring-buffer-design.md`?

message.content:
I did say the same address space, but that was wrong in the
general case. In a small embedded system that is the case. But
in a Linux system the processes should be able to share queue
ends and messages should zerocopy between processes.

We need to support inter & intro process communication!

content.text:
That correction is load-bearing, and it happily *simplifies* one earlier decision while adding a real control-plane problem. Implications:

**1. No pointers in any shared structure — offsets only.** With different virtual address mappings per process, everything that travels through a queue or lives in a message header must be position-independent: a message's pool provenance becomes `(pool id, offset)` rather than a back-pointer, and a descriptor is `(pool id, buffer index/offset)`. The pointer option from last turn is simply dead — which is fine, because the offset scheme works identically in the embedded single-address-space case (one region, offsets from its base). One design serves both worlds; the intra-process case is just the degenerate mapping where everyone shares the same base. The existing ring is already position-independent internally (header + slot array in one region, indexed not pointed-to), so the primitive needs nothing.

**2. Pool ids need a resolution step per process.** If a descriptor says "pool 7, offset 0x4c0," each process needs a local table mapping pool id → where *it* mapped that pool's memory. That's a small registry, but it implies pool ids are agreed upon across processes — either allocated by some coordinator, or derived from something intrinsic (e.g. the shm object's identity).

**3. "Any message via any queue" gets a mapping precondition.** Zero-copy across processes means the receiver reads the message bytes in place — so the receiving process must have the message's pool mapped. The honest statement of the requirement becomes: any message can travel over any queue *between processes that have mapped its pool*. That pushes a setup/control-plane question into scope: how a process discovers and maps a pool it hasn't seen — pre-arranged at init (embedded-friendly, allocation-free after startup), or on demand via fd-passing over a Unix socket (Linux-friendly, dynamic). Probably both, as profiles.

**4. The trust boundary hardens.** Cross-process, the peer may be buggy or hostile with respect to your address space: every offset and index arriving through shared memory must be bounds-checked against the receiver's own view of the region before use, and message contents are untrusted bytes (which `FromBytes` already models well). The crate has this instinct already — the `>=` occupancy check, the "store values, never addresses" note on the user words — it now becomes a systemwide rule.

The architecture that's emerged over these three turns: **ring (existing, unchanged) → queue-of-descriptors + pools-with-provenance → region registry + setup plane**, all offset-based, with the MPSC free-path question from last turn still open (per-freeer return rings vs. CAS free-list — cross-process actually strengthens the per-freeer-rings option, since a Treiber stack over untrusted shared memory is a scarier validation problem than bounded rings).

This is now enough accumulated design to be worth writing down before it drifts. Shall I draft it as a new section in `ring-buffer-design.md` — stated requirements first, then the decisions (offsets, provenance, mapping precondition) and open questions (free path, pool-id allocation, setup plane)?

content.text:
Noted — and the phasing is right: SPSC now, MPSC later. Two observations about how "later" should shape "now":

- **MPSC lands in the ring layer, not the layers above.** A multi-producer ring is a genuinely different protocol (the single `producer_idx` counter becomes CAS-claimed, and you need per-slot sequence/ready state — Vyukov-style — because producer 2 can finish before producer 1). That's a *sibling primitive* next to the existing SPSC ring, not a modification of it. The descriptor/pool/registry layers we've been designing don't care how many producers the underlying ring has — so nothing above the ring needs to anticipate MPSC beyond not hard-coding "exactly one producer" into names and header claims. Which touches your existing Todo #1 (endpoint claims word): the claim scheme should be "role slots" rather than "the one producer bit," so an MPSC ring can later claim N producer slots without a layout rethink.
- **MPSC would dissolve the free-path problem.** The pool's free path *is* an MPSC queue — many freers, one allocator. Today, with only SPSC available, per-freeer return rings are the workaround; once an MPSC ring exists, a pool's free-list becomes exactly one MPSC ring and the workaround retires. That's a nice convergence: the free-path pain is an argument *for* the MPSC roadmap item, and the design doc should record it as the intended endgame.

The requirement set now reads: zero-copy SPSC ring (done) → decoupled pools + descriptor queues → any-message-any-queue with pool provenance → offset-based, inter- and intra-process → MPSC ring as a future sibling primitive that also simplifies pool frees.

Any more requirements to state, or shall I write this up as the design section in `ring-buffer-design.md` (requirements, decisions, open questions) plus the matching Todo entries?

content.text:
A stack is the right call, and it actually resolves the free-path question better than either option I'd listed. Let me sharpen the three ideas in play:

**"Simply mark it available" implies searching.** A per-buffer available flag makes *free* trivial but moves the cost to *alloc*: the allocator has to scan for a set flag, which is O(pool size) and touches cold lines. The way to keep free cheap *and* alloc O(1) is for the free operation itself to be the marking — pushing the buffer onto a free-list. And the list can be **intrusive**: a freed buffer's own header holds the next-link (as an offset, per our no-pointers rule), so the free-list needs zero extra storage.

**LIFO's real win here is working-set size.** Your cache instinct is right, and it's worth noting *why* it beats the alternatives: a FIFO free-ring cycles through every buffer in the pool before reusing one — maximum working set, every allocation touches the coldest possible memory. A LIFO stack reuses the most-recently-freed buffer, so a steady-state producer/consumer pair churns through a handful of hot buffers instead of the whole pool. (One honest caveat for the written record: the freed buffer is hottest in the *freer's* cache, and the allocator is a different core — so cross-core you don't get the full same-thread malloc-style win, but the small-working-set effect holds regardless and also helps TLB.)

**A shared stack is still an MPSC object — but a friendly one.** My "the free path is an MPSC queue" was about the concurrency shape (many pushers, one popper), not about FIFO order; a stack satisfies it fine. Concretely it's a Treiber stack: free = CAS the head offset to your buffer; alloc = pop the head. Two properties make our case the *easy* case:

- **Single popper kills ABA.** The classic Treiber hazard needs concurrent poppers. With exactly one allocator popping (the pool owner), a node at head can't be removed out from under the popper's CAS — pushes just retry it. No hazard pointers, no epochs.
- **Validation is tractable.** Cross-process, the head and next-links are untrusted, so the popper bounds-and-alignment-checks each offset before following it. A corrupt peer can lose buffers or cycle the list (allocator should cap pops per attempt), but can't make the allocator read outside the pool.

Compared to per-freeer return rings, the stack is one structure instead of N, needs no freer registration, and gets the LIFO hot-reuse that rings can't. Cost: one CAS per free/alloc — contended only when frees genuinely collide, which SPSC-phase never does.

So the free path settles as: **intrusive LIFO free-stack per pool, offset-linked, single-popper Treiber, validated pops**. That replaces both earlier options, and it also decouples nicely from the MPSC-ring roadmap item — the pool no longer waits on it.

Ready to write the design section whenever you're done adding requirements.

Also, there isn't just one Memory Pool A naive notion is one per
process or per thread. But they could also be shared. Also, pools don't

content.text:
Most of that folds cleanly into what we already have — but one word in it, "shared," touches the single sharp edge in the free-stack design. Taking it in order:

**Heterogeneous pools are absorbed by provenance + self-description.** Since every message already carries `(pool id, offset)` provenance, nothing anywhere assumes pools look alike. Each pool's region starts with its own header recording its geometry — buffer size, buffer count, alignment, layout version — exactly like the ring header does today, validated on attach the same way. A descriptor traveling over a queue stays a uniform small entry regardless of how big the message behind it is; the receiver resolves the pool id and learns the geometry from the pool's header. Size classes (a process keeping a 64-byte pool and a 4 KiB pool and picking per message) become pure application policy — the crate doesn't need to know.

**Alignment is a per-pool validated invariant.** Buffer size a cache-line multiple, buffers cache-line aligned within the region, region base page-aligned (which mmap gives for free) — recorded in the pool header, checked at attach and by `check_type` against the message type, same discipline as the ring's slot geometry.

**Per-process / per-thread / shared is topology, not mechanism — except for allocation.** Here's the edge: the Treiber free-stack was easy because it had a *single popper*. That's what killed ABA. Shared **freeing** is already the design — any thread or process frees into any pool, that's the MPSC push side. But a pool shared for **allocating** means multiple poppers, and multi-popper Treiber over untrusted shared memory is the genuinely hard case (ABA needs tagged/generation-counted heads, and validation gets murkier).

So I'd state this as an invariant rather than a limitation: **every pool has exactly one owning allocator (a single thread) at a time; any party may free into it.** Per-thread and per-process pools satisfy it trivially, and "shared pool" remains meaningful — shared for reading, forwarding, and freeing, just not for concurrent alloc. A process wanting shared allocation capacity uses one pool per allocating thread, which is also the naive-and-good default you named. If a real need for multi-allocator pools ever shows up, it's a contained upgrade (generation-tagged head CAS) rather than a redesign — same spirit as MPSC-ring-later.

Requirement set updated: arbitrary many pools, arbitrary per-pool geometry (alignment-constrained, self-described, attach-validated), any topology of ownership — with the one-allocator-per-pool invariant as the proposed boundary. Flag it if you want shared allocation to be first-class instead; otherwise it goes in the doc as a decision.

content.text:
Agreed, and your complexity estimate is about right — it's a contained upgrade, not a redesign. Concretely, multi-allocator (multi-popper) alloc needs one thing the single-popper version doesn't: an ABA-proof head. The standard fix is packing the head as `(offset, generation)` in a single 64-bit word and CASing both together — every successful pop increments the generation, so a stale head that "looks the same" fails the CAS. Since offsets are 32-bit in this design anyway, the packed word fits native `AtomicU64` CAS on every 64-bit target, no double-width-CAS portability trouble. The validation story (bounds-check offsets, cap pop retries) carries over unchanged. Cost: alloc goes from uncontended-CAS to potentially-contended-CAS — which is exactly the price you'd expect for sharing, and only paid by pools that opt into it.

So the phasing mirrors the ring roadmap, and the doc records it as:

- **Phase 1 (now):** one owning allocator per pool; anyone frees. Per-thread pools are the performance default.
- **Phase 2 (eventually):** shared-allocation pools via generation-tagged head — for memory-constrained topologies where N per-thread pools waste too much, trading some alloc contention for footprint. Possibly a per-pool flag/variant rather than a global switch, so hot paths keep the cheap version.

And the memory-waste concern has a second mitigation worth a line in the doc: heterogeneous pools already let a thread keep a small private pool for its hot path and fall back to a big shared pool for bursts — sharing and per-thread aren't either/or once both exist.

That's the requirement set as I understand it, now internally consistent. Ready to write the design section and Todo entries whenever you say go.

1	# Ring buffer design
2	
3	Design notes for the zero-copy ring buffer. This file uses
4	[Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It covers requirements,
5	constraints, the memory layout, the API, and the validation
6	ladder, and is kept in sync with the implementation in
7	`src/` (as-built): `lib.rs` holds the region-level machinery
8	(header, init/attach/split, geometry helpers) and re-exports
9	the endpoint modules `producer.rs` (Producer, WriteSlot,
10	Full) and `consumer.rs` (Consumer, ReadSlot, Empty).
11	
12	## Goal
13	
14	A ring buffer for passing typed messages between a producer and
15	a consumer with no copying at the boundary: the producer writes
16	its message directly into buffer memory, the consumer reads it
17	in place. The same layout must work intra-process (threads) and
18	inter-process (shared memory).
19	
20	## Requirements
21	
22	- **no_std** — the crate builds with `#![no_std]` and no
23	  `alloc`: fixed capacity, operating over a caller-provided
24	  memory region. Suitable for embedded and kernel-adjacent use.
25	- **Zero-copy via zerocopy** — messages are structs implementing
26	  the [zerocopy](https://docs.rs/zerocopy) traits
27	  (`IntoBytes`, `FromBytes`, `KnownLayout`, `Immutable`); the
28	  buffer transmutes slots into and out of `&T`/`&mut T` with no
29	  serialization step and no `unsafe` at the call site.
30	- **IPC-suitable layout** — the control block (the
31	  producer/consumer indices) and the slot array live in one
32	  contiguous region that
33	  can be mapped into multiple address spaces:
34	  - `#[repr(C)]` explicit layout, stable across processes
35	    compiled from the same source.
36	  - No pointers inside the region — offsets and indices only.
37	- **SPSC first** — single producer, single consumer, lock-free
38	  via atomic producer/consumer indices. MPMC is out of scope
39	  for this cycle;
40	  the layout should not preclude adding it later.
41	- **In-place access API** — `reserve_slot` on either endpoint,
42	  then commit (producer) or release (consumer), exposing
43	  references into the buffer rather than returning values by
44	  move.
45	- **Safety** — all `unsafe` encapsulated inside the crate with
46	  documented invariants. The message path (reserve_slot, then
47	  commit / release) is fully safe; the one `unsafe` public
48	  entry
49	  is `Ring::attach` — see [API](#api) for why it cannot be
50	  made safe.
51	
52	## Constraints
53	
54	- **Slot geometry: M slots × N bytes** — a *slot* is one
55	  fixed-size buffer in the ring; the ring holds M of them.
56	  - M (capacity) is a power of two — index wrapping is a
57	    mask, no division; checked at construction.
58	  - N (slot size) is a multiple of the cache-line size, and
59	    each slot is cache-line aligned — adjacent slots never
60	    share a line, and any `T` with `align_of::<T>()` up to
61	    the line size fits without padding logic.
62	  - This trades space for isolation (a 16-byte message
63	    occupies a full 64-byte slot) — the right default for
64	    IPC; a packed-slot variant is a possible follow-on.
65	- **Monotonic indices** — producer/consumer indices are
66	  free-running unsigned counters (wrap via masking), so
67	  full/empty are distinguishable without a separate flag or
68	  a sacrificial slot.
69	- **Cache-line separation** — producer- and consumer-owned
70	  indices are padded/aligned to separate cache lines to avoid
71	  false sharing.
72	- **Typed access over untyped slots** — the slot geometry
73	  (M, N) is a property of the buffer, independent of any
74	  message type; a `T` is a zerocopy view into a slot, valid
75	  when `size_of::<T>() <= N` and `align_of::<T>()` divides
76	  the slot alignment (checked at construction or compile
77	  time). Variable-length framed messages are a possible
78	  follow-on, not in this cycle.
79	- **zerocopy, core-only** — depend on zerocopy with default
80	  features compatible with `no_std`; no other runtime
81	  dependencies.
82	
83	## Memory layout
84	
85	One contiguous `#[repr(C)]` region, mappable into multiple
86	address spaces: a `Header` struct followed by the slot array.
87	The `Header` spans four cache lines; each concurrently-written
88	index owns its own line, and alignment is expressed on exactly
89	the fields that need it via a `CacheAligned` wrapper (the
90	compiler computes the inter-line padding):
91	
92	```rust
93	/// Cache-line-aligned wrapper granting its field sole
94	/// ownership of the line.
95	#[repr(C, align(64))]
96	struct CacheAligned<T>(T);
97	
98	#[repr(C)]
99	struct Header {
100	    // line 0 — geometry; written by init (magic last), then
101	    // read-only. Cold: per-op paths use handle snapshots.
102	    magic: AtomicU32,          // layout marker; init/attach handshake
103	    layout_version: AtomicU32, // bumped on any layout change
104	    slot_size: AtomicU32,      // N in bytes, cache-line multiple
105	    capacity: AtomicU32,       // M, power of two; index mask is M - 1
106	    cache_line: AtomicU32,     // CACHE_LINE the region was built with
107	    // line 1 — producer-owned
108	    producer_idx: CacheAligned<AtomicU32>, // written by producer only
109	    // line 2 — consumer-owned
110	    consumer_idx: CacheAligned<AtomicU32>, // written by consumer only
111	    // line 3 — app-owned scratch; zeroed at init, never
112	    // touched by the crate again
113	    user: CacheAligned<[AtomicU32; 16]>,
114	}
115	```
116	
117	```
118	offset 0                    Header (256 bytes, 4 cache lines)
119	offset size_of::<Header>()  slots: M × N bytes
120	```
121	
122	- One type owns the whole control-block layout:
123	  `size_of::<Header>()` is the slot-array offset.
124	- **User line last, on purpose** — an app-side overrun walking
125	  forward out of `user` lands in slot 0 (the app's own message
126	  data), not in an index line.
127	- **`repr(align(N))` takes only an integer literal** — it
128	  cannot name `CACHE_LINE`, so the `64` is written out and
129	  const asserts tie `align_of::<CacheAligned<AtomicU32>>()`
130	  and `size_of::<Header>()` back to the constant.
131	- **Every field is atomic** — the region may be mapped by a
132	  peer at any time, so even "immutable" geometry must be free
133	  of data races; a peer scribbling on plain fields would be
134	  UB, on atomics it is merely garbage values. `AtomicU32` has
135	  the same layout as `u32`, so layout_version is unaffected.
136	- **Init/attach handshake** — init stores the geometry and
137	  indices first (`Relaxed`), then `magic` **last** with
138	  `Release`; attach loads `magic` **first** with `Acquire`. A
139	  peer that pre-mapped the region and observes MAGIC therefore
140	  also observes the geometry it validates. (Re-initializing a
141	  region a peer is already attached to stays outside the
142	  contract.)
143	- **Slots** — `M` slots of `N` bytes, each cache-line
144	  aligned (N is a line multiple, so alignment follows from
145	  the array base).
146	- **`CACHE_LINE = 64` plays two roles** — a layout-ABI
147	  constant and a false-sharing pad. On CPUs with 128-byte
148	  effective line pitch (Apple M-series; Intel's adjacent-line
149	  prefetcher is why crossbeam pads 128 on x86_64 too) the
150	  padding under-separates — a perf question for the bench
151	  work, not correctness. Since layout v2 the header records
152	  the build's `cache_line` and attach rejects a mismatch
153	  (`BadCacheLine`), so the constant can become per-target
154	  (cfg) or shrink for cache-less embedded tiers without any
155	  silent cross-build corruption.
156	- **Assume nothing beyond `AtomicU32` load/store** — the
157	  protocol uses no CAS/RMW, so it reaches
158	  load/store-only targets (e.g. `thumbv6m`/Cortex-M0).
159	  Features that want CAS (endpoint claims) should degrade or
160	  gate rather than raise the floor. 8/16-bit targets (AVR,
161	  MSP430 — no `AtomicU32`) would need index-width
162	  genericization; out of scope, recorded in Ideas.
163	
164	Index scheme (resolves the atomic-width question):
165	
166	- **Fixed-width `AtomicU32`, free-running**:
167	  - increments are wrapping adds, never masked in storage
168	  - fixed width keeps the layout identical for 32- and
169	    64-bit peers sharing the region
170	- **Masked only at slot access**:
171	  - slot position = `idx & (M - 1)`
172	- **Occupancy** = `producer_idx.wrapping_sub(consumer_idx)`
173	  (valid while `M <= 2^31`):
174	  - empty: `producer_idx == consumer_idx`
175	  - full: `producer_idx - consumer_idx == M` — implemented as
176	    `>= M`, so a peer-corrupted consumer_idx fails toward
177	    `Full` instead of handing out an unowned slot
178	  - no sacrificial slot
179	- **u32 wrap at `2^32` is harmless** because M is a power
180	  of two:
181	  - `2^32 % M == 0`, so occupancy math and masked slot
182	    positions both carry across the wrap unchanged
183	- **Example** (M = 4, `p` = producer_idx, `c` = consumer_idx):
184	  - start: `p = 0, c = 0` — occupancy 0, empty
185	  - producer commits 4 messages: `p = 4, c = 0` — occupancy
186	    4 == M, full; slot positions written were 0, 1, 2, 3
187	  - consumer releases 1: `p = 4, c = 1` — occupancy 3, one
188	    slot free; the next reserve_slot writes slot `4 & 3 == 0`
189	
190	Ordering: the producer loads `consumer_idx` with `Acquire`
191	and publishes `producer_idx` with `Release`; the consumer
192	mirrors (loads `producer_idx` `Acquire`, publishes
193	`consumer_idx` `Release`).
194	
195	## API
196	
197	Construction (resolves the foreign-memory question):
198	
199	- `Ring::init(region: &'a mut [u8], slot_size: u32, capacity:
200	  u32) -> Result<Ring<'a>, Error>` — validates geometry
201	  (power-of-two M, line-multiple N, alignment, region big
202	  enough — sized in u64, since the N×M product can wrap a
203	  32-bit usize), then writes the header per the
204	  [handshake](#memory-layout) (indices zeroed, magic last).
205	- `unsafe Ring::attach(region: *mut u8, len: usize) ->
206	  Result<Ring<'a>, Error>` — validates magic (Acquire),
207	  layout_version, and geometry against the region length,
208	  over the same shared `header_ptr` checks as init. Unsafe,
209	  deliberately, on two grounds:
210	  - zerocopy's shared validated casts require `Immutable`,
211	    which `Header` correctly is not (atomics) — so the cast
212	    is hand-verified rather than derive-verified;
213	  - only the caller can vouch that the pointer is a live,
214	    genuinely shared, writable mapping (e.g. `MAP_SHARED`)
215	    and that the SPSC role contract holds across processes.
216	- `ring.split() -> (Producer<'_>, Consumer<'_>)` — consumes
217	  the `Ring`, so each handle exists at most once per ring per
218	  process (SPSC discipline; cross-process there is one
219	  producer process and one consumer process by contract).
220	- **Geometry snapshot** — slot_size / capacity / mask are
221	  copied into `Ring` and the handles at init/attach; per-op
222	  paths never re-read peer-writable header fields. Combined
223	  with the all-atomic header, a hostile peer can degrade the
224	  ring to garbage messages or spurious Full/Empty — never UB.
225	
226	Producer — reserve_slot/commit, in place:
227	
228	- `producer.reserve_slot::<T>() ->
229	  Result<WriteSlot<'_, T>, Full>` — next free slot as `&mut T`
230	  (zerocopy `FromBytes + IntoBytes + KnownLayout`); `T`
231	  geometry is asserted against the slot size on each call (a
232	  mismatch is a programming error, so it panics; typed
233	  endpoints that check once are a follow-on — see
234	  [todo.md](todo.md)).
235	- `WriteSlot::commit(self)` — publishes the slot
236	  (`producer_idx + 1`, `Release`). Dropping without commit
237	  abandons the reservation (nothing published).
238	
239	Consumer — reserve_slot/release, in place:
240	
241	- `consumer.reserve_slot::<T>() ->
242	  Result<ReadSlot<'_, T>, Empty>` — oldest unread slot as
243	  `&T`.
244	- `ReadSlot::release(self)` — frees the slot
245	  (`consumer_idx + 1`, `Release`). Dropping without release
246	  leaves the slot unread — the next reserve_slot returns it
247	  again.
248	
249	Each side reserves at most **one slot at a time**: the guard
250	holds the endpoint's `&mut` borrow, so a second `reserve_slot`
251	while a guard is live is a compile error, not a runtime state.
252	
253	Both endpoints also expose `user() -> &[AtomicU32; 16]` — the
254	header's app-owned scratch line; see
255	[Blocking and user words](#blocking-and-user-words).
256	
257	Guard internals: both guards hold **raw slot pointers** and
258	mint `&T` / `&mut T` per access via Deref. A reference *field*
259	would be argument-protected (noalias) for the entire
260	`commit`/`release` call, but the store inside that call hands
261	the slot to the other side, which may access it while the
262	protector is still live — an aliasing race Miri flags
263	(found in 0.3.0-6 by the threaded test).
264	
265	Batch release (resolves the batching question): deferred —
266	the guard API leaves room for a `release_n(n)` later; the
267	single-slot guard is the whole surface this cycle.
268	
269	## Blocking and user words
270	
271	The crate never blocks and never spins. `reserve_slot`
272	returning [`Full`]/[`Empty`] immediately is the entire API;
273	what to do about it — spin, yield, park, await — is policy,
274	and policy belongs to the layer above (an embedded caller may
275	WFE on an interrupt, an async runtime wants a waker, a pinned
276	busy-poll thread wants a pure spin; no single crate-blessed
277	hybrid fits all three).
278	
279	What the crate provides is mechanism: the header's `user`
280	line — 16 `AtomicU32` words, zeroed by `Ring::init`, exposed
281	via `user()` on both endpoints, and never read, written, or
282	interpreted by the crate afterwards. It is the well-known
283	shared-memory home where independently written peers can
284	build a wakeup protocol (waiter flags, futex words, sequence
285	numbers). `AtomicU32` deliberately: futex-shaped, portable to
286	targets without 64-bit atomics, race-free under concurrent
287	peer access.
288	
289	Contracts and cautions for that layer:
290	
291	- **Lost-wakeup discipline** — the sleep side must re-check
292	  the ring *after* publishing its "wake me" flag and before
293	  sleeping (`set flag → reserve_slot once more → sleep`), and
294	  the wake side checks the flag *after* its commit/release
295	  (`commit → check flag → wake`). Skipping the re-check races
296	  a commit landing between flag-set and sleep: the waker saw
297	  no flag, the sleeper saw no message — it sleeps forever.
298	  futex-style primitives exist precisely to make the final
299	  check-and-sleep atomic.
300	- **Values, not addresses** — a pointer stored in the region
301	  is meaningless in the peer's address space (and a
302	  dereference of peer-writable bytes besides). Store data;
303	  keep pointers on your side of the wall.
304	- **Timeouts** — a peer can die while you sleep; every wait
305	  should carry a timeout so a dead producer degrades the
306	  consumer to periodic polling, not a hang.
307	
308	## Validation
309	
310	Per-commit, alongside the cargo cycle:
311	
312	- `cargo +nightly miri test` — the full suite, threaded test
313	  included (its message count is reduced under `cfg(miri)`;
314	  interpreted spin loops are slow). Miri has caught two real
315	  Stacked Borrows violations: the init retag (0.3.0-4) and
316	  the guard argument-protector race (0.3.0-6). The latter
317	  reproduces only when the threaded test runs, so the full
318	  suite — not a non-threaded subset — stays in the ladder.
319	- `indices_survive_u32_wrap` pins the free-running-index wrap
320	  argument (fill/drain across `u32::MAX` with masked
321	  positions intact).

# Ring buffer design

Design notes for the zero-copy ring buffer. This file uses
[Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It covers requirements,
constraints, the memory layout, the API, and the validation
ladder, and is kept in sync with the implementation in
`src/` (as-built): `lib.rs` holds the region-level machinery
(header, init/attach/split, geometry helpers) and re-exports
the endpoint modules `producer.rs` (Producer, WriteSlot,
Full) and `consumer.rs` (Consumer, ReadSlot, Empty).

## Goal

A ring buffer for passing typed messages between a producer and
a consumer with no copying at the boundary: the producer writes
its message directly into buffer memory, the consumer reads it
in place. The same layout must work intra-process (threads) and
inter-process (shared memory).

## Requirements

- **no_std** — the crate builds with `#![no_std]` and no
  `alloc`: fixed capacity, operating over a caller-provided
  memory region. Suitable for embedded and kernel-adjacent use.
- **Zero-copy via zerocopy** — messages are structs implementing
  the [zerocopy](https://docs.rs/zerocopy) traits
  (`IntoBytes`, `FromBytes`, `KnownLayout`, `Immutable`); the
  buffer transmutes slots into and out of `&T`/`&mut T` with no
  serialization step and no `unsafe` at the call site.
- **IPC-suitable layout** — the control block (the
  producer/consumer indices) and the slot array live in one
  contiguous region that
  can be mapped into multiple address spaces:
  - `#[repr(C)]` explicit layout, stable across processes
    compiled from the same source.
  - No pointers inside the region — offsets and indices only.
- **SPSC first** — single producer, single consumer, lock-free
  via atomic producer/consumer indices. MPMC is out of scope
  for this cycle;
  the layout should not preclude adding it later.
- **In-place access API** — `reserve_slot` on either endpoint,
  then commit (producer) or release (consumer), exposing
  references into the buffer rather than returning values by
  move.
- **Safety** — all `unsafe` encapsulated inside the crate with
  documented invariants. The message path (reserve_slot, then
  commit / release) is fully safe; the one `unsafe` public
  entry
  is `Ring::attach` — see [API](#api) for why it cannot be
  made safe.

## Constraints

- **Slot geometry: M slots × N bytes** — a *slot* is one
  fixed-size buffer in the ring; the ring holds M of them.
  - M (capacity) is a power of two — index wrapping is a
    mask, no division; checked at construction.
  - N (slot size) is a multiple of the cache-line size, and
    each slot is cache-line aligned — adjacent slots never
    share a line, and any `T` with `align_of::<T>()` up to
    the line size fits without padding logic.
  - This trades space for isolation (a 16-byte message
    occupies a full 64-byte slot) — the right default for
    IPC; a packed-slot variant is a possible follow-on.
- **Monotonic indices** — producer/consumer indices are
  free-running unsigned counters (wrap via masking), so
  full/empty are distinguishable without a separate flag or
  a sacrificial slot.
- **Cache-line separation** — producer- and consumer-owned
  indices are padded/aligned to separate cache lines to avoid
  false sharing.
- **Typed access over untyped slots** — the slot geometry
  (M, N) is a property of the buffer, independent of any
  message type; a `T` is a zerocopy view into a slot, valid
  when `size_of::<T>() <= N` and `align_of::<T>()` divides
  the slot alignment (checked at construction or compile
  time). Variable-length framed messages are a possible
  follow-on, not in this cycle.
- **zerocopy, core-only** — depend on zerocopy with default
  features compatible with `no_std`; no other runtime
  dependencies.

## Memory layout

One contiguous `#[repr(C)]` region, mappable into multiple
address spaces: a `Header` struct followed by the slot array.
The `Header` spans four cache lines; each concurrently-written
index owns its own line, and alignment is expressed on exactly
the fields that need it via a `CacheAligned` wrapper (the
compiler computes the inter-line padding):

```rust
/// Cache-line-aligned wrapper granting its field sole
/// ownership of the line.
#[repr(C, align(64))]
struct CacheAligned<T>(T);

#[repr(C)]
struct Header {
    // line 0 — geometry; written by init (magic last), then
    // read-only. Cold: per-op paths use handle snapshots.
    magic: AtomicU32,          // layout marker; init/attach handshake
    layout_version: AtomicU32, // bumped on any layout change
    slot_size: AtomicU32,      // N in bytes, cache-line multiple
    capacity: AtomicU32,       // M, power of two; index mask is M - 1
    cache_line: AtomicU32,     // CACHE_LINE the region was built with
    // line 1 — producer-owned
    producer_idx: CacheAligned<AtomicU32>, // written by producer only
    // line 2 — consumer-owned
    consumer_idx: CacheAligned<AtomicU32>, // written by consumer only
    // line 3 — app-owned scratch; zeroed at init, never
    // touched by the crate again
    user: CacheAligned<[AtomicU32; 16]>,
}
```

```
offset 0                    Header (256 bytes, 4 cache lines)
offset size_of::<Header>()  slots: M × N bytes
```

- One type owns the whole control-block layout:
  `size_of::<Header>()` is the slot-array offset.
- **User line last, on purpose** — an app-side overrun walking
  forward out of `user` lands in slot 0 (the app's own message
  data), not in an index line.
- **`repr(align(N))` takes only an integer literal** — it
  cannot name `CACHE_LINE`, so the `64` is written out and
  const asserts tie `align_of::<CacheAligned<AtomicU32>>()`
  and `size_of::<Header>()` back to the constant.
- **Every field is atomic** — the region may be mapped by a
  peer at any time, so even "immutable" geometry must be free
  of data races; a peer scribbling on plain fields would be
  UB, on atomics it is merely garbage values. `AtomicU32` has
  the same layout as `u32`, so layout_version is unaffected.
- **Init/attach handshake** — init stores the geometry and
  indices first (`Relaxed`), then `magic` **last** with
  `Release`; attach loads `magic` **first** with `Acquire`. A
  peer that pre-mapped the region and observes MAGIC therefore
  also observes the geometry it validates. (Re-initializing a
  region a peer is already attached to stays outside the
  contract.)
- **Slots** — `M` slots of `N` bytes, each cache-line
  aligned (N is a line multiple, so alignment follows from
  the array base).
- **`CACHE_LINE = 64` plays two roles** — a layout-ABI
  constant and a false-sharing pad. On CPUs with 128-byte
  effective line pitch (Apple M-series; Intel's adjacent-line
  prefetcher is why crossbeam pads 128 on x86_64 too) the
  padding under-separates — a perf question for the bench
  work, not correctness. Since layout v2 the header records
  the build's `cache_line` and attach rejects a mismatch
  (`BadCacheLine`), so the constant can become per-target
  (cfg) or shrink for cache-less embedded tiers without any
  silent cross-build corruption.
- **Assume nothing beyond `AtomicU32` load/store** — the
  protocol uses no CAS/RMW, so it reaches
  load/store-only targets (e.g. `thumbv6m`/Cortex-M0).
  Features that want CAS (endpoint claims) should degrade or
  gate rather than raise the floor. 8/16-bit targets (AVR,
  MSP430 — no `AtomicU32`) would need index-width
  genericization; out of scope, recorded in Ideas.

Index scheme (resolves the atomic-width question):

- **Fixed-width `AtomicU32`, free-running**:
  - increments are wrapping adds, never masked in storage
  - fixed width keeps the layout identical for 32- and
    64-bit peers sharing the region
- **Masked only at slot access**:
  - slot position = `idx & (M - 1)`
- **Occupancy** = `producer_idx.wrapping_sub(consumer_idx)`
  (valid while `M <= 2^31`):
  - empty: `producer_idx == consumer_idx`
  - full: `producer_idx - consumer_idx == M` — implemented as
    `>= M`, so a peer-corrupted consumer_idx fails toward
    `Full` instead of handing out an unowned slot
  - no sacrificial slot
- **u32 wrap at `2^32` is harmless** because M is a power
  of two:
  - `2^32 % M == 0`, so occupancy math and masked slot
    positions both carry across the wrap unchanged
- **Example** (M = 4, `p` = producer_idx, `c` = consumer_idx):
  - start: `p = 0, c = 0` — occupancy 0, empty
  - producer commits 4 messages: `p = 4, c = 0` — occupancy
    4 == M, full; slot positions written were 0, 1, 2, 3
  - consumer releases 1: `p = 4, c = 1` — occupancy 3, one
    slot free; the next reserve_slot writes slot `4 & 3 == 0`

Ordering: the producer loads `consumer_idx` with `Acquire`
and publishes `producer_idx` with `Release`; the consumer
mirrors (loads `producer_idx` `Acquire`, publishes
`consumer_idx` `Release`).

## API

Construction (resolves the foreign-memory question):

- `Ring::init(region: &'a mut [u8], slot_size: u32, capacity:
  u32) -> Result<Ring<'a>, Error>` — validates geometry
  (power-of-two M, line-multiple N, alignment, region big
  enough — sized in u64, since the N×M product can wrap a
  32-bit usize), then writes the header per the
  [handshake](#memory-layout) (indices zeroed, magic last).
- `unsafe Ring::attach(region: *mut u8, len: usize) ->
  Result<Ring<'a>, Error>` — validates magic (Acquire),
  layout_version, and geometry against the region length,
  over the same shared `header_ptr` checks as init. Unsafe,
  deliberately, on two grounds:
  - zerocopy's shared validated casts require `Immutable`,
    which `Header` correctly is not (atomics) — so the cast
    is hand-verified rather than derive-verified;
  - only the caller can vouch that the pointer is a live,
    genuinely shared, writable mapping (e.g. `MAP_SHARED`)
    and that the SPSC role contract holds across processes.
- `ring.split() -> (Producer<'_>, Consumer<'_>)` — consumes
  the `Ring`, so each handle exists at most once per ring per
  process (SPSC discipline; cross-process there is one
  producer process and one consumer process by contract).
- **Geometry snapshot** — slot_size / capacity / mask are
  copied into `Ring` and the handles at init/attach; per-op
  paths never re-read peer-writable header fields. Combined
  with the all-atomic header, a hostile peer can degrade the
  ring to garbage messages or spurious Full/Empty — never UB.

Producer — reserve_slot/commit, in place:

- `producer.reserve_slot::<T>() ->
  Result<WriteSlot<'_, T>, Full>` — next free slot as `&mut T`
  (zerocopy `FromBytes + IntoBytes + KnownLayout`); `T`
  geometry is asserted against the slot size on each call (a
  mismatch is a programming error, so it panics; typed
  endpoints that check once are a follow-on — see
  [todo.md](todo.md)).
- `WriteSlot::commit(self)` — publishes the slot
  (`producer_idx + 1`, `Release`). Dropping without commit
  abandons the reservation (nothing published).

Consumer — reserve_slot/release, in place:

- `consumer.reserve_slot::<T>() ->
  Result<ReadSlot<'_, T>, Empty>` — oldest unread slot as
  `&T`.
- `ReadSlot::release(self)` — frees the slot
  (`consumer_idx + 1`, `Release`). Dropping without release
  leaves the slot unread — the next reserve_slot returns it
  again.

Each side reserves at most **one slot at a time**: the guard
holds the endpoint's `&mut` borrow, so a second `reserve_slot`
while a guard is live is a compile error, not a runtime state.

Both endpoints also expose `user() -> &[AtomicU32; 16]` — the
header's app-owned scratch line; see
[Blocking and user words](#blocking-and-user-words).

Guard internals: both guards hold **raw slot pointers** and
mint `&T` / `&mut T` per access via Deref. A reference *field*
would be argument-protected (noalias) for the entire
`commit`/`release` call, but the store inside that call hands
the slot to the other side, which may access it while the
protector is still live — an aliasing race Miri flags
(found in 0.3.0-6 by the threaded test).

Batch release (resolves the batching question): deferred —
the guard API leaves room for a `release_n(n)` later; the
single-slot guard is the whole surface this cycle.

## Blocking and user words

The crate never blocks and never spins. `reserve_slot`
returning [`Full`]/[`Empty`] immediately is the entire API;
what to do about it — spin, yield, park, await — is policy,
and policy belongs to the layer above (an embedded caller may
WFE on an interrupt, an async runtime wants a waker, a pinned
busy-poll thread wants a pure spin; no single crate-blessed
hybrid fits all three).

What the crate provides is mechanism: the header's `user`
line — 16 `AtomicU32` words, zeroed by `Ring::init`, exposed
via `user()` on both endpoints, and never read, written, or
interpreted by the crate afterwards. It is the well-known
shared-memory home where independently written peers can
build a wakeup protocol (waiter flags, futex words, sequence
numbers). `AtomicU32` deliberately: futex-shaped, portable to
targets without 64-bit atomics, race-free under concurrent
peer access.

Contracts and cautions for that layer:

- **Lost-wakeup discipline** — the sleep side must re-check
  the ring *after* publishing its "wake me" flag and before
  sleeping (`set flag → reserve_slot once more → sleep`), and
  the wake side checks the flag *after* its commit/release
  (`commit → check flag → wake`). Skipping the re-check races
  a commit landing between flag-set and sleep: the waker saw
  no flag, the sleeper saw no message — it sleeps forever.
  futex-style primitives exist precisely to make the final
  check-and-sleep atomic.
- **Values, not addresses** — a pointer stored in the region
  is meaningless in the peer's address space (and a
  dereference of peer-writable bytes besides). Store data;
  keep pointers on your side of the wall.
- **Timeouts** — a peer can die while you sleep; every wait
  should carry a timeout so a dead producer degrades the
  consumer to periodic polling, not a hang.

## Validation

Per-commit, alongside the cargo cycle:

- `cargo +nightly miri test` — the full suite, threaded test
  included (its message count is reduced under `cfg(miri)`;
  interpreted spin loops are slow). Miri has caught two real
  Stacked Borrows violations: the init retag (0.3.0-4) and
  the guard argument-protector race (0.3.0-6). The latter
  reproduces only when the threaded test runs, so the full
  suite — not a non-threaded subset — stays in the ladder.
- `indices_survive_u32_wrap` pins the free-running-index wrap
  argument (fill/drain across `u32::MAX` with masked
  positions intact).
- [loom](https://docs.rs/loom) for exhaustive ordering
  exploration is a possible follow-on (Ideas).

## Resolved questions

- **Cross-process trust** — resolved in 0.3.0-4: the
  all-atomic header plus the geometry snapshot mean a hostile
  peer cannot cause UB, only garbage messages (still valid
  `T`s — zerocopy guarantees representation, not sense) or
  spurious Full/Empty. `attach` remains `unsafe` for the
  mapping-liveness contract, not for peer trust.
- **`split()` once-guard** — direction chosen: an
  endpoint-claims word in the header (CAS to claim the
  producer / consumer role, second claimant gets an error),
  which also catches in-process double-attach; costs a
  layout_version bump (or spends `_pad0`). Promoted to a
  `## Todo` entry in [todo.md](todo.md); the documented
  contract stands until then.

## Messaging layer: pools and descriptor queues

Design for the layer above the ring — not yet built; the
sections above remain the as-built record of the ring itself.

The ring's `reserve_slot` fuses three acts that a messaging
system needs separated: it allocates message memory (the slot),
assigns queue position (the reservation is the ring head), and
opens a publication window (the guard exclusively borrows the
endpoint, blocking all other sends). Getting a message must not
imply sending it now — or ever. The fix is a layer, not a ring
rework: pools own message memory, queues carry small
descriptors, and the existing ring is the unchanged primitive
underneath (reserve-to-commit becomes a momentary act, where
the fusion is harmless).

### Layer requirements

- **Decoupled get/hold/send/free** — allocate a message, hold
  it indefinitely, send it over any queue later, forward it,
  or free it unsent.
- **Any quantity of queues and pools** in a system, and any
  message can travel over any queue.
- **Inter- and intra-process** — processes share queue ends,
  and message payloads zero-copy across process boundaries.
  The embedded single-address-space case is the degenerate
  mapping where all peers share one base.
- **Heterogeneous pools** — pools differ arbitrarily in buffer
  size and count; each meets the alignment constraints and
  self-describes its geometry.
- **Phasing** — queues are SPSC rings now, an MPSC ring is a
  future sibling primitive; pools have a single allocator
  now, shared allocation eventually.

### Provenance and descriptors

- **Offsets only, everywhere** — different per-process
  mappings mean no pointers in any shared structure; this
  also serves the single-address-space case unchanged.
- **Message provenance** — every buffer begins with a small
  header naming its pool `(pool id, offset)`; whoever holds a
  message last must free it to the right pool, so provenance
  travels with the message.
- **Descriptors** — a queue entry is a uniform small
  `(pool id, buffer offset)` regardless of message size; the
  receiver resolves the pool id and learns geometry from the
  pool's own header.
- **Pool self-description** — each pool region starts with a
  header (magic, layout version, buffer size, count,
  alignment), attach-validated exactly like the ring header.
- **Pool-id resolution** — each process keeps a local registry
  mapping pool id to its own mapping of that pool's region.
  "Any message over any queue" carries the precondition that
  both endpoints have the message's pool mapped.
- **Trust** — offsets and indices arriving through shared
  memory are untrusted: bounds- and alignment-checked against
  the receiver's own view before use, same discipline as the
  ring's geometry snapshot.

### Free path: intrusive LIFO free-stack

Freeing must be cheap for many freers and allocation must be
O(1) with no searching (a per-buffer available-flag makes free
trivial but alloc a scan). The free operation is itself the
marking: push the buffer onto the pool's free-stack.

- **Intrusive** — a freed buffer's header holds the next-link
  as an offset; the free-list needs no extra storage.
- **LIFO on purpose** — we think a stack beats a free-ring on
  cache behavior: FIFO cycles through every buffer (maximum
  working set), LIFO reuses the most-recently-freed few, and
  the small working set also helps the TLB. The full
  same-thread malloc-style hot-reuse win is diluted because
  the freer and allocator are different cores; the
  working-set effect stands regardless.
- **Single-popper Treiber stack** — free = CAS the head to
  your buffer's offset (MPSC push side: any thread or process
  frees into any pool); alloc = the one owning allocator pops
  the head. A single popper eliminates classic Treiber ABA:
  no node leaves the list under a competing pop, pushes just
  retry the CAS.
- **Validated pops** — head and next-links are untrusted;
  the popper bounds/alignment-checks each offset and caps
  pops per attempt, so a corrupt peer can lose buffers or
  cycle the list but never make the allocator read outside
  the pool.
- **CAS note** — the free-stack is the first structure to
  need CAS; the ring protocol itself stays load/store-only,
  so the atomic floor rises only for pool users.

### Pool topology and phasing

- **One owning allocator per pool** (invariant, phase 1) — a
  single thread allocates; any party frees. Per-thread pools
  are the performance default and satisfy this trivially.
- **Shared pools already exist in phase 1** — shared for
  reading, forwarding, and freeing; only allocation is
  single-owner.
- **Shared allocation (phase 2)** — per-thread pools cost
  memory; N threads sharing one pool's capacity needs
  multi-popper alloc, which needs an ABA-proof head:
  pack `(offset, generation)` in one `AtomicU64` and CAS
  both together. A contained per-pool variant (opt-in flag),
  not a redesign; hot paths keep the cheap single-popper
  version. Heterogeneity mitigates meanwhile: a small private
  hot-path pool plus a big shared fallback pool.
- **MPSC ring (future sibling)** — multi-producer queues need
  a different ring protocol (CAS-claimed producer index,
  per-slot sequence state); it slots in as a sibling
  primitive under the same descriptor layer. The endpoint
  claims word should model role slots, not a single producer
  bit, so N producer claims fit later without a layout
  rethink.

### Open questions

- **Pool-id allocation** — coordinator-assigned versus derived
  from the shm object's identity.
- **Setup plane** — how a process discovers and maps a pool it
  hasn't seen: pre-arranged at init (embedded-friendly,
  allocation-free after startup) versus fd-passing over a
  Unix socket (Linux-friendly, dynamic); likely both, as
  profiles.
- **Message header shape** — minimum provenance is
  `(pool id, offset)`; whether length or a type tag joins it
  is undecided.

## Resolved questions

- **MPSC ring (future sibling)** — multi-producer queues need
  a different ring protocol (CAS-claimed producer index,
  per-slot sequence state); it slots in as a sibling
  primitive under the same descriptor layer. The endpoint
  claims word should model role slots, not a single producer
  bit, so N producer claims fit later without a layout
  rethink.

- **Pool-id allocation** — coordinator-assigned versus derived
  from the shm object's identity.
- **Setup plane** — how a process discovers and maps a pool it
  hasn't seen: pre-arranged at init (embedded-friendly,
  allocation-free after startup) versus fd-passing over a
  Unix socket (Linux-friendly, dynamic); likely both, as
  profiles.
- **Message header shape** — minimum provenance is
  `(pool id, offset)`; whether length or a type tag joins it
  is undecided.

+## Messaging layer: pools and descriptor queues
+Design for the layer above the ring — not yet built; the
+sections above remain the as-built record of the ring itself.
+The ring's `reserve_slot` fuses three acts that a messaging
+system needs separated: it allocates message memory (the slot),
+assigns queue position (the reservation is the ring head), and
+opens a publication window (the guard exclusively borrows the
+endpoint, blocking all other sends). Getting a message must not
+imply sending it now — or ever. The fix is a layer, not a ring
+rework: pools own message memory, queues carry small
+descriptors, and the existing ring is the unchanged primitive
+underneath (reserve-to-commit becomes a momentary act, where
+the fusion is harmless).
+### Layer requirements
+- **Decoupled get/hold/send/free** — allocate a message, hold
+  it indefinitely, send it over any queue later, forward it,
+  or free it unsent.
+- **Any quantity of queues and pools** in a system, and any
+  message can travel over any queue.
+- **Inter- and intra-process** — processes share queue ends,
+  and message payloads zero-copy across process boundaries.
+  The embedded single-address-space case is the degenerate
+  mapping where all peers share one base.
+- **Heterogeneous pools** — pools differ arbitrarily in buffer
+  size and count; each meets the alignment constraints and
+  self-describes its geometry.
+- **Phasing** — queues are SPSC rings now, an MPSC ring is a
+  future sibling primitive; pools have a single allocator
+  now, shared allocation eventually.
+### Provenance and descriptors
+- **Offsets only, everywhere** — different per-process
+  mappings mean no pointers in any shared structure; this
+  also serves the single-address-space case unchanged.
+- **Message provenance** — every buffer begins with a small
+  header naming its pool `(pool id, offset)`; whoever holds a
+  message last must free it to the right pool, so provenance
+  travels with the message.
+- **Descriptors** — a queue entry is a uniform small
+  `(pool id, buffer offset)` regardless of message size; the
+  receiver resolves the pool id and learns geometry from the
+  pool's own header.
+- **Pool self-description** — each pool region starts with a
+  header (magic, layout version, buffer size, count,
+  alignment), attach-validated exactly like the ring header.
+- **Pool-id resolution** — each process keeps a local registry
+  mapping pool id to its own mapping of that pool's region.
+  "Any message over any queue" carries the precondition that
+  both endpoints have the message's pool mapped.
+- **Trust** — offsets and indices arriving through shared
+  memory are untrusted: bounds- and alignment-checked against
+  the receiver's own view before use, same discipline as the
+  ring's geometry snapshot.
+### Free path: intrusive LIFO free-stack
+Freeing must be cheap for many freers and allocation must be
+O(1) with no searching (a per-buffer available-flag makes free
+trivial but alloc a scan). The free operation is itself the
+marking: push the buffer onto the pool's free-stack.
+- **Intrusive** — a freed buffer's header holds the next-link
+  as an offset; the free-list needs no extra storage.
+- **LIFO on purpose** — we think a stack beats a free-ring on
+  cache behavior: FIFO cycles through every buffer (maximum
+  working set), LIFO reuses the most-recently-freed few, and
+  the small working set also helps the TLB. The full
+  same-thread malloc-style hot-reuse win is diluted because
+  the freer and allocator are different cores; the
+  working-set effect stands regardless.
+- **Single-popper Treiber stack** — free = CAS the head to
+  your buffer's offset (MPSC push side: any thread or process
+  frees into any pool); alloc = the one owning allocator pops
+  the head. A single popper eliminates classic Treiber ABA:
+  no node leaves the list under a competing pop, pushes just
+  retry the CAS.
+- **Validated pops** — head and next-links are untrusted;
+  the popper bounds/alignment-checks each offset and caps
+  pops per attempt, so a corrupt peer can lose buffers or
+  cycle the list but never make the allocator read outside
+  the pool.
+- **CAS note** — the free-stack is the first structure to
+  need CAS; the ring protocol itself stays load/store-only,
+  so the atomic floor rises only for pool users.
+### Pool topology and phasing
+- **One owning allocator per pool** (invariant, phase 1) — a
+  single thread allocates; any party frees. Per-thread pools
+  are the performance default and satisfy this trivially.
+- **Shared pools already exist in phase 1** — shared for
+  reading, forwarding, and freeing; only allocation is
+  single-owner.
+- **Shared allocation (phase 2)** — per-thread pools cost
+  memory; N threads sharing one pool's capacity needs
+  multi-popper alloc, which needs an ABA-proof head:
+  pack `(offset, generation)` in one `AtomicU64` and CAS
+  both together. A contained per-pool variant (opt-in flag),
+  not a redesign; hot paths keep the cheap single-popper
+  version. Heterogeneity mitigates meanwhile: a small private
+  hot-path pool plus a big shared fallback pool.
+- **MPSC ring (future sibling)** — multi-producer queues need
+  a different ring protocol (CAS-claimed producer index,
+  per-slot sequence state); it slots in as a sibling
+  primitive under the same descriptor layer. The endpoint
+  claims word should model role slots, not a single producer
+  bit, so N producer claims fit later without a layout
+  rethink.
+### Open questions
+- **Pool-id allocation** — coordinator-assigned versus derived
+  from the shm object's identity.
+- **Setup plane** — how a process discovers and maps a pool it
+  hasn't seen: pre-arranged at init (embedded-friendly,
+  allocation-free after startup) versus fd-passing over a
+  Unix socket (Linux-friendly, dynamic); likely both, as
+  profiles.
+- **Message header shape** — minimum provenance is
+  `(pool id, offset)`; whether length or a type tag joins it
+  is undecided.

# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

_No cycle currently in progress._

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries
 live in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). Use the
 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).

1. Endpoint claims word: CAS-claimed producer/consumer roles
   in the ring header so a second attach/split claimant gets
   an error instead of silently violating SPSC; costs a
   layout_version bump (or spends `_pad0`)
   [details](ring-buffer-design.md#resolved-questions).
2. Typed endpoints: `Producer<T>` / `Consumer<T>` validating
   `T`'s geometry once at split instead of asserting on every
   reserve_slot [details](ring-buffer-design.md#api).

## Ideas

- Perf benches live in
  [iiac-perf](https://github.com/winksaville/iiac-perf)
  (sibling repo `../iiac-perf`), not here — its calibrated
  harness compares zc-ring against mpsc et al. directly
  (`zcring-1t`/`zcring-2t` mirroring `mpsc_1t`/`mpsc_2t`).
  An in-repo bench only if per-commit regression tracking
  proves necessary.

- Blocking layer above the crate (futex, eventfd, async
  wakers) built on the header's user line — mechanism and
  contracts in
  [Blocking and user words](ring-buffer-design.md#blocking-and-user-words);
  possibly a companion wrapper crate so independent peers
  share one protocol.
- loom-based exhaustive ordering exploration of the SPSC
  protocol.
- Polish: `Error` implements `Display` + `core::error::Error`;
  `occupancy()` / `is_empty()` accessors.
- Packed-slot variant (drop the cache-line-multiple slot
  constraint) for small-message space efficiency.
- Per-target / configurable `CACHE_LINE` (128 for Apple
  M-series false sharing, tiny for cache-less MCUs) — safe
  since attach validates the header's `cache_line`; decide
  values from iiac-perf measurements.
- Embedded floor: protocol is atomic load/store only (no
  CAS), so thumbv6m works today; keep it that way where
  possible (endpoint claims wants CAS — gate it), and 8/16-bit
  targets would need index-width genericization.
- Shared `Geometry` struct (`slot_size`, `capacity`, `mask`)
  held by Ring and passed whole to the endpoint
  constructors, slimming their signatures and Ring's fields.
- Black-box test split: move the public-API protocol tests
  (roundtrip, abandoned guards, threaded stress) to
  `tests/protocol.rs`; white-box tests (u32 wrap, attach
  header internals) stay in lib.rs. Do it when a trybuild
  compile-fail harness lands there too (pins the
  "second reserve_slot does not compile" guarantee).

and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.

message.content: Another future feature, provide a capability of adding a message to a FIFO if the ring is full. The obvious structure is a linked list with embedded "pointers" so that there are no allocations needed to append the message to the FIFO.

content.text:
Good feature — and it composes beautifully with what's already designed: the free-stack gave every buffer header an embedded next-link offset, and a buffer is only ever in *one* state at a time (free, held, in flight, or pending), so that same link field carries the overflow FIFO for free. Appending truly allocates nothing. There's also a pleasant hidden property: since messages come from pools, the "unbounded" FIFO is naturally bounded by total pool capacity — backpressure doesn't vanish, it reappears as allocation failure instead of queue-Full.

Let me add it to the design section and the Ideas list.

input.new_string:
### Overflow FIFO (future)

When a queue's ring is Full, the sender appends the message to
a pending FIFO instead of failing — "send" then always
succeeds while pool memory lasts.

- **Intrusive, zero-allocation** — the FIFO links through the
  same embedded next-link offset the free-stack uses. A
  buffer is on at most one list at a time (free stack,
  pending FIFO, in flight in a ring, held by an owner), so
  one link field serves every state.
- **Sender-private** — the pending list belongs to the
  producer endpoint: head + tail offsets for O(1) append, no
  shared mutation, no CAS. Order discipline: the producer
  drains the FIFO oldest-first before ring-sending anything
  new, or FIFO order breaks. Draining happens on subsequent
  send attempts and/or an explicit flush.
- **Validated traversal** — the links live in shared pool
  memory a peer can scribble; the drainer bounds- and
  alignment-checks each offset as the free-stack popper does.
- **Naturally bounded** — messages come from pools, so the
  FIFO cannot outgrow total pool capacity; backpressure
  reappears as allocation failure rather than queue Full.

### Open questions

# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

_No cycle currently in progress._

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries
 live in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). Use the
 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).

1. Message pools: decouple "get a message" from "send it" —
   self-describing arbitrary-geometry pools, offset-based
   `(pool id, offset)` provenance in a per-buffer header,
   intrusive LIFO free-stack (single-popper Treiber, validated
   pops); one owning allocator per pool for now
   [details](ring-buffer-design.md#messaging-layer-pools-and-descriptor-queues).
2. Descriptor queues: carry `(pool id, buffer offset)`
   descriptors over the existing SPSC ring so any message
   travels over any queue; per-process pool registry to
   resolve ids
   [details](ring-buffer-design.md#messaging-layer-pools-and-descriptor-queues).
3. Endpoint claims word: CAS-claimed producer/consumer roles
   in the ring header so a second attach/split claimant gets
   an error instead of silently violating SPSC; costs a
   layout_version bump (or spends `_pad0`)
   [details](ring-buffer-design.md#resolved-questions).
4. Typed endpoints: `Producer<T>` / `Consumer<T>` validating
   `T`'s geometry once at split instead of asserting on every
   reserve_slot [details](ring-buffer-design.md#api).

## Ideas

- Perf benches live in
  [iiac-perf](https://github.com/winksaville/iiac-perf)
  (sibling repo `../iiac-perf`), not here — its calibrated
  harness compares zc-ring against mpsc et al. directly
  (`zcring-1t`/`zcring-2t` mirroring `mpsc_1t`/`mpsc_2t`).
  An in-repo bench only if per-commit regression tracking
  proves necessary.

- Blocking layer above the crate (futex, eventfd, async
  wakers) built on the header's user line — mechanism and
  contracts in
  [Blocking and user words](ring-buffer-design.md#blocking-and-user-words);
  possibly a companion wrapper crate so independent peers
  share one protocol.
- loom-based exhaustive ordering exploration of the SPSC
  protocol.
- Polish: `Error` implements `Display` + `core::error::Error`;
  `occupancy()` / `is_empty()` accessors.
- Packed-slot variant (drop the cache-line-multiple slot
  constraint) for small-message space efficiency.
- Per-target / configurable `CACHE_LINE` (128 for Apple
  M-series false sharing, tiny for cache-less MCUs) — safe
  since attach validates the header's `cache_line`; decide
  values from iiac-perf measurements.
- Embedded floor: protocol is atomic load/store only (no
  CAS), so thumbv6m works today; keep it that way where
  possible (endpoint claims wants CAS — gate it), and 8/16-bit
  targets would need index-width genericization.
- Shared `Geometry` struct (`slot_size`, `capacity`, `mask`)
  held by Ring and passed whole to the endpoint
  constructors, slimming their signatures and Ring's fields.
- Black-box test split: move the public-API protocol tests
  (roundtrip, abandoned guards, threaded stress) to
  `tests/protocol.rs`; white-box tests (u32 wrap, attach
  header internals) stay in lib.rs. Do it when a trybuild
  compile-fail harness lands there too (pins the
  "second reserve_slot does not compile" guarantee).

## Done

Completed tasks are moved from `## Todo` to here, `## Done`, as they are completed
and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.

- refactor: ring buffer symmetric reserve_slot API [[2]]
- docs: commit-and-push is not a review waiver [[3]]
- refactor: ring buffer endpoint modules [[4]]
- feat: ring buffer user line + blocking contract [[5]]

I found several relevant Rust crates and resources for zero-copy inter-process communication with SPSC ring buffers:

## Key Crates Found:

**1. ipmpsc**
This library provides a type-safe, high-performance inter-process channel implementation based on a shared memory ring buffer. It uses bincode for (de)serialization, including zero-copy deserialization, making it ideal for messages with large &str or &[u8] fields. The ring buffer is backed by a shared memory-mapped file.

**2. ringbuffer-spsc**
A fast #[no_std] single-producer single-consumer (SPSC) ring buffer designed for low-latency and high-throughput scenarios. This crate is #![no_std] and uses alloc internally; it provides a minimal, ergonomic API that works well from no_std contexts that supply an allocator as well as from normal std programs.

**3. rtrb**
A wait-free single-producer single-consumer (SPSC) ring buffer for Rust. This crate can be used without the standard library (#![no_std]) by disabling the std feature (which is enabled by default), but the alloc crate is needed nevertheless.

**4. shmem-ipc** (successor to fdringbuf-rs)
This crate contains essentially the same ringbuffer implementation and contains more building blocks for setting the ringbuffer up between untrusted processes. The code gives you direct access to the ringbuffer memory (no memory copies).

All of these solutions support the zero-copy, SPSC, and inter-process communication requirements you're looking for.

Here are the search results for iceoryx2 and its zero-copy shared memory IPC implementation:

## Overview

iceoryx2 is based on a service-oriented architecture (SOA) and facilitates seamless inter-process communication (IPC). It is written in safe Rust, with first-class bindings for other languages.

## Zero-Copy Communication Mechanism

iceoryx2 is a decentralized, service-based inter-process communication (IPC) library designed for mission-critical, high-efficiency systems that achieves low latency and high throughput using a communication paradigm known as zero-copy communication.

Publishers produce data directly into shared memory, subscribers receive only an offset which they resolve into local pointers, guaranteeing true zero-copy communication.

## Memory Pools and Loan Mechanism

In iceoryx2 the memory of the data segment is managed by a pool allocator that partitions memory into fixed-size regions, which avoids fragmentation and allows predictable allocation times - essential for real-time and safety-critical systems.

Publishers loan uninitialized memory (SampleMutUninit) which gets written and sent as SampleMut, while subscribers receive immutable Sample references to the same memory location.

iceoryx2 provides a mechanism to loan memory and perform in-place initialization—something akin to C++'s placement new.

## Messaging Patterns

iceoryx2 supports publish-subscribe, events, request-response, and upcoming features like pipelines and blackboard.

content.text:
Mostly yes — one crate covers almost the entire list — but not in the shape this project is aiming for. The short version: **iceoryx2 is the existing crate that does what you've described; nothing existing does it at this project's embedded floor and size.**

**iceoryx2** ([GitHub](https://github.com/eclipse-iceoryx/iceoryx2), [iceoryx.io](https://iceoryx.io/)) is a Rust-native zero-copy IPC library, and the capability overlap with our design is striking, point by point:

- **Decoupled get/send** — its core API is exactly the decoupling you identified: a publisher *loans* an uninitialized sample from a pool, writes it in place, and sends it later — or never (dropping releases it). Loan and send are separate acts.
- **Pools + descriptors** — data segments are managed by a pool allocator of fixed-size regions; what travels between processes is an offset that the receiver resolves against its own mapping — the same offsets-not-pointers conclusion we reached.
- **Inter- and intra-process zero-copy** — its whole reason to exist; publishers write into shared memory, subscribers read in place.
- **Beyond our roadmap** — it already has the things we filed as future: multi-producer patterns, an event/wakeup mechanism (our "blocking layer" idea), request-response, dynamic service discovery (our "setup plane" open question).

So the honest answer is: if the goal were simply to *have* this capability on Linux today, you'd use iceoryx2. Where it doesn't match this project:

- **It's a middleware, not a primitive.** Service-oriented architecture, discovery, naming, configuration — a large dependency surface. Our design is a small crate of composable primitives (ring, pool, descriptor queue) you assemble yourself.
- **No `no_std` / embedded floor.** iceoryx2 targets Linux/macOS/Windows/QNX-class platforms. Our ring runs today on `thumbv6m` with nothing but atomic load/store, and the design deliberately keeps CAS confined to the pool layer. Nothing in the ecosystem spans "Cortex-M0 to Linux shared memory" with one layout.
- **Trust posture differs.** Our attach-validated, hostile-peer-cannot-cause-UB discipline is a per-crate design commitment; iceoryx2's model assumes cooperating participants within its framework.

The partial matches confirm the gap: [rtrb](https://github.com/mgeier/rtrb) and [ringbuffer-spsc](https://crates.io/crates/ringbuffer-spsc) are SPSC rings but intra-process and `alloc`-dependent; [ipmpsc](https://crates.io/crates/ipmpsc) is shared-memory but serializes with bincode (not true zero-copy typed access); [shmem-ipc](https://github.com/diwic/fdringbuf-rs) is a byte ring between untrusted processes but has no typed messages, pools, or any-message-any-queue; `bbqueue`/`heapless` cover embedded but single-address-space only.

My take: the project remains justified on its own terms — the *combination* (tiny, `no_std`, load/store-only floor, attach-validated hostile-peer safety, layered pools/queues) is unoccupied territory. But iceoryx2 is worth deliberate study before implementing Todo #1 — its loan/send API and pool-offset machinery are a battle-tested version of exactly what we sketched, and stealing its lessons is cheaper than rediscovering them. Worth a Reference/Ideas note in the repo if you agree.

Sources: [iceoryx2 GitHub](https://github.com/eclipse-iceoryx/iceoryx2), [iceoryx.io](https://iceoryx.io/), [ekxide: implementing true zero-copy communication](https://ekxide.io/blog/how-to-implement-zero-copy-communication/), [iceoryx2 FAQ](https://github.com/eclipse-iceoryx/iceoryx2/blob/main/FAQ.md), [rtrb](https://github.com/mgeier/rtrb), [ipmpsc](https://crates.io/crates/ipmpsc), [ringbuffer-spsc](https://crates.io/crates/ringbuffer-spsc), [shmem-ipc / fdringbuf](https://github.com/diwic/fdringbuf-rs)
